coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots and their application as a fluorometric sensor for highly selective determination of 6-mercaptopurine in biological samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Saboorizadeh, Bahar
Zare-Dorabei, Rouholah
Shahbazi, Neda
coredata.dc:description:
Background 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) is an anticancer drug used in the treatment of a variety of cancers. Regarding its wide application, rapid and accurate detection of 6-MP is of great interest. Conventional techniques have significant drawbacks such as complex sample preparation, time-consuming analysis, and being expensive. On the other hand, fluorometric sensors have been used vastly to detect various chemical and biological analytes.
Methods Hydrothermal technique synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using a facile and environmentally benign approach using sweet potato as the carbon source. It was observed that in the presence of the 6-MP drug, the fluorescence emission of CQDs was enhanced. At first, the effect of important parameters (pH, temperature, and the CQDs volume ratio) on the fluorescence emission intensity was studied. Then the fluorometric sensor was used to determine 6-MP in different concentration ranges and complex solutions.
Significant findings The developed sensor showed promising results for the determination of 6-MP in the linear range of 0.4–5.4 mg L−1 and the detection limit of 0.01 mg L−1. The results were repeatable even in complex solutions in the presence of possible interfering species.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Carbon quantum dots
Mercaptopurine
Hydrothermal method
Green chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Standard procedure for the measurement of 6-MP
Optimization of the sensor response for the measurement of 6-MP
Real sample analysis and interfering study
Determination of 6-MP drug using the synthesized CQDs
The effect of CQDs volume ratio

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2021 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV-Vis spectra of the synthesized CQDs (a). Fluorescence emission from CQDs using different excitation wavelengths (300 – 400 nm) (b). (c) and (d) show the TEM image and particle size distribution of the synthesized CQDs, respectively.

FT-IR spectra of CQD before (blue) and after (red) conjugation with the 6-MP drug (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Fluorescence emission from CQDs in the presence of 5.4 mg L−1 of the 6-MP drug. Panel (b–d) show the effect of temperature, pH, and CQD volume ratio on the fluorescence emission, respectively.
Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs in the presence of the 6-MP drug in the concentration range of 0.4–5.4 mg L−1 (a). Panel (b) shows the calibration curve for the response of the sensor.
Intraday (a) and interday (b) response of the developed sensor in the presence of 60 mg L−1 of the 6-MP drug.
The changes in the fluorescence emission (ΔF) from CQDs in the presence of the 6-MP drug and some potential interferents (1000 mg L−1).
Analytical results (N = 5) of 6-MP in plasma and urine samples.
Comparison of the current work with other report approaches for detecting 6 –MP.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots and their application as a fluorometric sensor for highly selective determination of 6-mercaptopurine in biological samples

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry and Micro and Nano Extraction, Department of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry and Micro and Nano Extraction
Iran University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Research Laboratory of Spectrometry and Micro and Nano Extraction, Department of Chemistry, Iran University of Science and Technology, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) is an anticancer drug used in the treatment of a variety of cancers. Regarding its wide application, rapid and accurate detection of 6-MP is of great interest. Conventional techniques have significant drawbacks such as complex sample preparation, time-consuming analysis, and being expensive. On the other hand, fluorometric sensors have been used vastly to detect various chemical and biological analytes.

Hydrothermal technique synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) using a facile and environmentally benign approach using sweet potato as the carbon source. It was observed that in the presence of the 6-MP drug, the fluorescence emission of CQDs was enhanced. At first, the effect of important parameters (pH, temperature, and the CQDs volume ratio) on the fluorescence emission intensity was studied. Then the fluorometric sensor was used to determine 6-MP in different concentration ranges and complex solutions.

The developed sensor showed promising results for the determination of 6-MP in the linear range of 0.4–5.4 mg L−1 and the detection limit of 0.01 mg L−1. The results were repeatable even in complex solutions in the presence of possible interfering species.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer is a global health problem, a leading cause of death worldwide, and a significant threat to human health [1–3]. Chemotherapy is used as the primary approach for cancer disease treatment which has specific drawbacks, such as destruction of the immune system [4]. Therefore, finding a new, biocompatible, and sensitive treatment method for cancer is of a high importance. Simultaneously, rapid and accurate determination of anticancer drugs in human serum is a crucial concern. 6-mercaptopurine (6-MP) is a purine anticancer drug and one of the major gradients of antineoplastic agents in chemotherapy systems which has promising effects on suppressing cancers, bowel infection, and acute lymphoblastic diseases treatment.

The under dosage of this drug leads to adverse effects on the treatment process, while it is over dosage leads to bone marrow suppression and liver problems. Therefore, the 6-MP diagnostic sensors have been the subject of intense interest in the past several years. Conventionally, mass spectroscopy, capillary electrophoresis, Raman spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, electrochemical techniques, and high-performance liquid chromatography have been used to determine 6-MP [5–10]. However, practical applications of most of these techniques are limited due to their disadvantages, such as being tedious, time-consuming, and the need for expensive instruments.

In the meantime, optical sensors have been used as an emerging determination method of various chemical and biochemical analytes [11–13]. Among them, fluorescent sensors based on fluorescence emission are at the center of attention. Carbon nanomaterials (e.g., quantum dots and nanotubes) are well-known for their bright and stable fluorescence emission [14–16]. Luminescence carbon quantum dots (CQDs) possess unique properties such as high biocompatibility, low toxicity, high photostability, low molecular weight, and solubility in water [17,18]. In this regard, carbon quantum dots were used to fabricate accurate sensors to determine a wide range of analytes [19–22].

In this paper, CQDs are synthesized via a green approach and used as a fluorescent probe for rapid and straight forward detection of the 6-MP drug in aqueous solutions. This is the first report on the fluorometric detection of 6-MP using carbon quantum dots to the best of our knowledge. The effect of experimental conditions on the sensing efficiency was thoroughly studied. The developed sensor detected the 6-MP drug in human plasma and urine samples with promising reproducibility in the optimized condition.

High purity Acetic acid (CH3COOH), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), and boric acid (H3BO3) were purchased from Sigma-Alrich and used to prepare the Britton-Robinson buffer. Sodium hydroxide, NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, and NaF were purchased from Merck in analytical grade. Methanol, fructose, glucose, and l-ascorbic acid were procured from Sigma-Aldrich. Double-distilled water was used for preparing the stock solutions.

Sweet potatoes (from local markets in Tehran, Iran) are used as the carbon source in the hydrothermal synthesis of CQDs, according to a previous report [23]. In summary, 100 gr of fresh sweet potatoes were chopped and added to 350 ml of double-distilled water. The mixture was blended with a high-speed mixer while heating the mixture for 3 h at 100 °C by a magnetic stirrer. After that, it was transferred to an autoclave and kept at 180 °C for 8 h; after cooling to room temperature, it was centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 RPM to separate the coarse particles from the solution.

The fluorescence spectra were measured using a Teifsang FL-Ar-2015 spectrometer. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was used for characterizing and determining CQDs morphology by a Philips-SCM120 TEM operating at 100 kV. Zeta potential and hydrodynamic size were measured using a Zetasizer device (Nano SZ100, HORIBA Instrument, Japan). The UV-Vis spectrophotometry was used to obtain the absorption spectra by a PG T80 + UV/Vis spectrophotometer. An Avatar FT-IR spectrophotometer was used to identify CQD functional groups. The pH was set and determined by utilizing the pH meter Mettier-S220.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Standard procedure for the measurement of 6-MP
ce:section.ce:para: The following procedure was used to measure the 6-MP drug using the synthesized CQD [23]. About 0.5 mL of Britton-Robinson buffer (pH = 3) and 50 μL of CQD were mixed. Next, 1.5 mL of double-distilled water was added to dilute the mixture. Then, different amounts of the 6-MP solution were transferred by micropipette to reach the final concentration in the range of 0.4–5.4 mg L−1. The fluorescence emission spectra of the solutions were recorded in the range of 400–600 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the sensor response for the measurement of 6-MP
ce:section.ce:para: According to the preliminary experiments, the temperature of the CQDs + 6-MP solution, pH, and the CQDs/drug volume ratio significantly affected the fluorescence emission of the developed sensor. In this regard, the impact of these parameters was studied to optimize the sensor's response. The effect of pH was studied on the prepared Britton-Robinson buffer. The buffer (total volume of 1000 mL) was made from a mixture of CH3COOH (2.36 mL), H3PO4 (2.71 mL), and H3BO3 (2.74 g). The pH of this solution was tuned in the range of 3–9 using NaOH (0.2 M). The effect of temperature was studied by controlling the temperature of the CQDs+drug solution in the range of 30–50 °C. The effect of CQDs' volume ratio was assessed by examining the sensor's response in different final volume ratios of CQDs in the range of 0.03–0.05.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real sample analysis and interfering study
ce:section.ce:para: The real sample analysis was performed in human urine and plasma to demonstrate the practical application of the developed sensor using previously published protocols [8]. Briefly, 100 µL of plasma and 1 mL of the 6-MP drug in various concentrations were mixed inside the microtube and diluted using double-distilled water. The same protocol was used for urine, except the 50 times dilution of the urine before starting the sample preparation. The recovery tests were implemented to validate the performance of the developed sensor. The ability of the sensor to determine the 6-MPdrug in complex mixtures was assessed by recording the response of the sensor in the presence of possible interferents. For this study, NaCl, KCl, glucose, CaCl2, vitamin C, MgCl2, fructose, NaF, lysine, AlCl3, and CuSO4 in the final concentration of 1000 mg L−1 were added, and the sensor's response for the determination of 6-MP was recorded.

The UV-Vis spectra of CQDs are shown in Fig. 1 (a). The CQDs illustrated an optical absorption in the UV area, with a sequence prolonged to the visible area. The absorption peak at 450 nm is attributed to the π→π* transition of C=C. The maximum emission peak of CQDs is 500 nm. According to Fig. 1(b), the P.L. emission peak of CQDs redshifted (varying from 310 to 410 nm) with the excitation wavelength. An explanation for this displacement is the size difference of the particles and the distribution of fluorescent active sites on the CQDs surface. Smaller particles are excited at lower wavelengths, and larger particles are excited at higher wavelengths. The mentioned size distribution can be seen in the TEM images and the size distribution diagram (Fig. 1(c) and (d)).

The surface chemistry of CQDs before and after drug conjugation was studied using FT-IR spectroscopy. According to the obtained data for CQD in Fig. 2 , peaks at 3301 cm−1, 2930 cm−1, and 1667 cm−1 can be attributed to the O-H stretching vibration, C-H stretching vibration, and C=O bond, respectively. The N-H stretches of amines can be seen in the 3000–3300 cm−1 region. In addition, the band at 1590 cm−1 can be attributed to the bending vibrations of C=C. The observed bands at 1396 cm−1, 1195 cm−1, 1024 cm−1 were assigned to C-H, C-OH, and C-O stretching vibrations, respectively [23,24]. According to the obtained results, the presence of amine, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups was confirmed on the surface of the CQDs. After conjugation with the 6-MP drug, significant changes can be observed in the spectrum, especially in the range of 1000–1700 cm−1. The intense peaks around 1020 cm−1 and 1610 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of the C-N and C=N bonds, respectively. The presence of these bands confirmed the successful conjugation of the 6-MP drug with the CQDs [25]. It can be inferred that the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups played a crucial role in the interaction of CQD and 6-MP and the formation of C-N and N-O bonds.

Figs. S1 and S2 present the zeta potential measurements from CQDs and 6-MP. The zeta potential for the pristine CQDs was -1.0 mV. This negative sign represents the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface. However, by adding the 6-MP drug, the zeta potential increased to 5.5 mV, indicating the proper interaction of the sensor with the analyte. The hydrodynamic diameter obtained from the DLS measurement was 6.8 nm (Fig. S3), consistent with their obtained results from TEM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of 6-MP drug using the synthesized CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3 (a), even in the presence of a trace amount of 6-MP drug, significant changes occurred in the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs. As mentioned earlier, preliminary experiments showed that the temperature, pH, and CQDs volume ratio played a crucial role in the developed sensor's final response. In this regard, these three parameters' effect was studied in the presence of 60 mg L−1 6-MP.

A certain amount of the diluted CQDs (50 µL) was heated at different temperatures (30, 35, 40, 45, and 50 °C) in a microtube, and the fluorescence emission in the presence of 6-MP drug was monitored. According to the obtained results (Fig. 3(b)), maximum fluorescence emission was observed at 35 °C.

The pH of the buffer solution used in the sensing experiment was adjusted using NaOH 0.2 M, and the fluorescence emission of the sample was monitored. Fig. 3(c) shows that the maximum fluorescence emission was obtained at pH = 3, which can be attributed to the better interaction of the 6-MP drug with CQDs. It was observed that the intensity of CQD emission decreased. Then, by reaching pH3, the emission increased. This process can be explained by the fact that as the pH decreases and the environment becomes acidic, the hydroxyl group of CQD becomes protonated, increasing the active sites of CQD, which naturally interacts with more drug species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of CQDs volume ratio
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of CQDs volume ratio was also studied in the range of 0.03–0.05 while the total volume of the sample is 2000 μL. According to the obtained results (Fig. 3(d)), the optimal value for this ratio was 0.05. In this regard, for further sensing experiments, the pH of the solution was fixed at three while the temperature and CQD volume were set at 35 °C and 50 μL, respectively.

After optimizing the sensor's response, the sensor's performance in the presence of various concentrations of the 6-MP was investigated (Fig. 4 (a)). As shown from Fig. 4(b), the maximum fluorescence emission has a linear relation with the 6-MP in the concentration range of 0.4–5.4 mg L−1. The limit of detection (LOD) for this sensor was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
LOD =
3 SD
m
Where S.D. is the standard deviation of the blank sample and m is the slope of the calibration curve. The LOD of the developed sensor was calculated as 0.01 mg L−1.

The ability of the developed sensor to detect 6-MP in real samples was evaluated by spiking a known amount of 6-MP into human plasma and urine samples. According to the obtained results of RSD and recovery tests (Table 1 ), the designed optical sensor was reliable for the analysis of 6-MP in practical applications.

As previously confirmed by the FT-IR results, the 6-MP drug can attach to CQDs' surface via carboxyl, amine, and hydroxyl groups. Such conjugation decreased the interparticle distance between CQDs and led to the phenomenon called aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement. Such aggregation-induced fluorescence enhancement was previously observed in different nanostructures [28–30]. The extent of this aggregation and the related fluorescence enhancement is proportional to the concentration of 6-MP in the media, thus making it possible to detect this drug via fluorescence emission measurement. To compare the obtained analytical performance with the previous reports for the determination of 6-MP, the obtained dynamic ranges and LOD values were summarized in Table 2 . As can be seen, the developed fluorometric sensor can determine the 6-MP drug accurately using a straightforward approach.

In this study, fluorescent CQDs were synthesized using a green and facile method and used as a fluorometric sensor to determine 6-MP drug in the aqueous and biological samples. The impact of important parameters (pH, volume ratio of CQDs, and temperature) on the determination process was thoroughly investigated. The developed sensor exhibited numerous advantages such as reproducibility, selectivity, and high sensitivity compared to previously developed techniques for the determination of 6-MP. The developed fluorometric sensor detected 6-MP in the concentration range of 0.4–5.4 mg L−1 with the LOD of 0.01 mg L−1 even in the complex solutions. The recovery values of the spiked samples were in the range of 88–120%, which confirmed the practical application of the developed sensor based on CQDs. Using such a green methodology to synthesize nanostructures and their application in sensing platforms seems to be the future direction of this field.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
On behalf of all authors, the corresponding author declares that there is no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The financial support of this study by the Iran University of Science and Technology is gratefully acknowledged. The author acknowledges financial support from the Iran National Science Foundation (INSF).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.jtice.2021.09.015.
